name: Mirror Light Reflection
description: Players rotate or place reflectors so a simulated light path reaches receivers, filters, or targets.
design_notes: Mirror-light puzzles need deterministic ray routing, receiver ids, occlusion, snap increments, and debug visualization. Designers should know when rays update, how many bounces are allowed, whether mirrors can be moved while active, and how the solved state is saved.
design_purpose: Create line-of-sight puzzles that make spatial angles and occlusion visible.
Let players manipulate receivers, mirrors, filters, and blockers to complete a circuit.
Support layered rooms where one beam can affect doors, hazards, or color channels.
player_fantasy: The player feels like they are aligning a precise optical machine and watching the room respond.

parameters.name: reflection_ray_id
parameters.description: Stable light path identifier used by receivers, effects, and save data.

parameters.name: mirror_instance_id
parameters.description: Reflector object that changes ray direction through rotation or placement.

parameters.name: light_receiver_id
parameters.description: Receiver that reacts when the correct ray or channel reaches it.

parameters.name: rotation_snap_degrees
parameters.description: Angular increment used to make mirror alignment readable and reproducible.

parameters.name: max_bounce_count
parameters.description: Maximum reflections evaluated before the ray stops to avoid runaway loops.

parameters.name: occlusion_layer_mask
parameters.description: Collision layers that can block or interrupt the light path.

edge_cases: A mirror rotates while the raycast update is evaluating later bounces.
Two mirrors create a loop that repeatedly hits the same reflectors.
A moving blocker interrupts a beam after the receiver has already opened a door.
Ray visuals and receiver state update at different cadences and briefly disagree.
common_bugs: Floating-point drift makes a visually aligned mirror miss the receiver by a tiny angle.
Raycasts hit the mirror's back face or decorative mesh instead of the intended reflective plane.
Receivers remain active after the ray is blocked because deactivation events are not emitted.
Save/load restores mirror rotation but not active receiver state or beam color.
balancing_notes: Snap increments make optical puzzles easier to reason about than free rotation.
Long bounce chains should expose intermediate beam segments so players can locate the mistake.
Receivers that require color channels or filters should reveal that requirement before players brute force angles.
accessibility_notes: Use beam thickness, receiver labels, sound, and icons in addition to color.
Offer reduced glare and high-contrast beam rendering for players sensitive to bright effects.

implementation_notes.unity: Use Physics.Raycast or 2D raycasts from a reflection_ray_id source, reflecting across mirror normals with a bounded max_bounce_count. Store mirror_instance_id rotations and receiver active state, and draw debug segments for level authors.
implementation_notes.godot: Use PhysicsRayQueryParameters2D or 3D ray queries and reflector nodes with snapped rotation. Receiver nodes should subscribe to a ray evaluation result rather than polling visuals.
implementation_notes.unreal: Line traces can evaluate beam segments, while mirror actors expose a reflective plane and snap increment. Replicate mirror rotation or receiver state if co-op players can manipulate the puzzle.
implementation_notes.web: Use deterministic vector math and segment intersection for 2D puzzles, or Three.js raycasters for 3D. Keep visual beam meshes derived from the current ray path and clear stale receiver ids on every evaluation.

relationships.reason: Colored light channels can deepen mirror puzzles when receivers require specific symbols or colors.
Light reflection puzzles need non-color receiver labels or contrast-safe palettes.
Top-down or fixed cameras can make reflection paths and receiver positions easier to parse.

example_games.title: The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time
example_games.note: Uses light reflection and mirror alignment in temple puzzles.

example_games.title: The Talos Principle
example_games.note: Uses beam routing and receivers as core puzzle elements.
